---
title: "greenland"
subtitle: "3D offline benchmark — test_greenland.x"
---

## What it tests

`test_greenland.x` runs elsa on a real ice sheet: the Greenland domain at 16 km,
forced offline from a Yelmo restart
(`data/initmip-grl-16km/yelmo_restart.nc`, the same file the
[tracer](https://github.com/fesmc/tracer) package uses, so the two schemes can
be compared on identical host fields).

The restart carries no spun-up isochrone field, so there is no known answer to
compare against. This is a **structural** check — against a real velocity field,
real staggering, real non-uniform sigma levels, and a real ice margin — rather
than a comparison with a closed-form solution.

Source: [`src/test_greenland.f90`](https://github.com/fesmc/elsa/blob/main/src/test_greenland.f90).
Run it with `make run-greenland`; it writes `output/GRL-16KM/elsa.nc`.

## Staggering, in practice

Yelmo writes `ux`/`uy` on staggered acx/acy nodes. elsa's advection wants face
velocities — which is exactly what acx/acy nodes are — so they are handed over
as-is with `stagger = "acx_acy"` and nothing is destaggered. The `tracer`
package reads the same file and *does* destagger to cell centres, because it
wants cell-centred velocities. The contrast is the whole point of expressing
staggering as a coordinate rather than a conversion.

## What is checked

Every update, on both a native (`grid_factor = 1`) and a coarsened
(`grid_factor = 2.5`) run:

- layer thicknesses stay finite and non-negative;
- every column sums to the host's ice thickness (closure error $0$ at
  `grid_factor = 1`, $\sim10^{-15}$ coarsened);
- ice-free columns carry no layers;
- isochrones stay ordered — younger ice sits higher, and none escapes the ice
  column;
- at `grid_factor = 1`, the conservative map reproduces the host ice thickness
  exactly and conserves volume;
- reseeding of emptied margin columns stays rare (a handful of thin cells per
  step, out of ~7000).

## The output

![Left: the depth below the surface of the oldest isochrone, across the ice sheet. Right: the full layer stack along a transect, coloured by isochrone age, with the ice surface in black. The right panel is the view the isochronal scheme exists to produce — the layers a model would compare against radiostratigraphy.](../figures/greenland_isochrones.png){#fig-greenland}

## Restart is bit-identical

The benchmark also runs the restart round-trip: a continuous run, and the same
run stopped halfway, written to a restart file, and picked up again. The two end
with

$$
\max_{i,j,k}\bigl|\,d^{\text{restart}}_{ijk} - d^{\text{continuous}}_{ijk}\,\bigr| = 0.
$$

Bit-identical, not merely close. That is the only check strong enough to catch a
piece of state — `n_top`, the isochrone schedule, the previous-step ice
thickness — being *reconstructed* rather than *carried*. See
[Design](../design.qmd#restart) for what the restart stores and why `d_iso`
must be written in double precision.

## Performance

At 16 km (19 186 cells, 20 layers) a 2000-year run spends 0.38 s in
`elsa_update` on one core, and 0.15 s on eight. Cost is linear in simulated time
and in layer count, and the total sub-step count does not depend on the coupling
period. Extrapolating to the Rieckh et al. (2024) control configuration — 160
kyr, ~415 layers on average — is of order 20 minutes on a single core, against
the ~24 h of ELSA time implied by that paper's Fig. 9. (Different machines, so
treat it as an order-of-magnitude comparison.)
